rules.message: `any` type used — defeats TypeScript's type checking for this value. Replace with a concrete type, a generic parameter, or `unknown` plus a type guard if the shape is genuinely dynamic. Suppress a vetted case with `// any-ok`.

`as any` / `as unknown as` cast — a hard escape from type checking (`as any` disables it, `as unknown as X` force-casts through an incompatible type). Prefer a type guard, a properly-typed API, or `unknown` plus a narrowing check. Plain `as X` to a compatible type is not flagged. Suppress a vetted case with `// as-ok`.

useEffect callback declared async — React ignores the returned Promise and no cleanup function can be returned; a rejection becomes unhandled. Move the async work into an inner async function or IIFE: `useEffect(() => { void doAsync(); }, deps)`. Suppress a vetted case with `// unhandled-promise-ok`.

An `on<Event>={async ...}` JSX handler (or `on<Event>: async ...` handler-map property) has an `await` but no try/catch — a rejection becomes an unhandled promise rejection. Add try/catch (or `.catch()`) inside the handler. Suppress a vetted case with `// async-handler-ok`.

A decimal-fraction or negative-exponent numeric literal compared with `==`/`===`/`!=`/`!==` — IEEE754 floats don't represent most decimal fractions exactly, so equality on a computed value is unreliable even against an exactly-written literal (`0.1 + 0.2 !== 0.3`; SonarQube's S1244 flags all float equality for the same reason). Use an epsilon comparison (`Math.abs(a - b) < eps`), integer minor units, or a decimal library instead. Money-named comparisons (`price`/`amount`/`balance`/`fee`/`cost`) are covered separately by `be-db/float-money-compare` and are not double-flagged here. Suppress a vetted case with `// float-eq-ok`.

A constant-result comparison: `x === NaN`/`x == NaN` is always false and `x !== NaN`/`x != NaN` is always true (NaN never equals anything, including itself — use `Number.isNaN(x)`), and strict `x === []`/`x === {}` compares object identity against a freshly-allocated literal, so it is always false (`!==` always true — use `.length === 0` / `Object.keys(x).length === 0`). Loose `x == []` is deliberately NOT matched (coercion can make it true — `0 == []` is true). Core ESLint's `use-isnan` covers the NaN half when ESLint is configured — zzop's value here is catching it with zero lint config. Suppress a vetted case with `// always-false-ok`.

A numeric-looking string literal compared with `<`/`>`/`<=`/`>=` — string comparison is lexicographic, not numeric (`'10' < '9'` is `true`; `'10.0.0' < '9.0.0'` is `true`). Convert both sides with `Number(...)` first, or use a semver library for version strings. Suppress a vetted case with `// numeric-string-cmp-ok`.

.toFixed() returns a STRING — using its result in `-`/`*`/`/`/`%` arithmetic silently coerces back to a number and loses the rounding/formatting intent (string concatenation via `+` is a separate, usually-intentional display case and is NOT flagged here). Do the arithmetic first and call `.toFixed()` last, for display only, or wrap in `Number(x.toFixed(2))` if re-quantizing is genuinely intended. Suppress a vetted case with `// tofixed-arith-ok`.

A timezone/epoch pitfall in `Date` handling: a date-only ISO string (`new Date('2024-01-15')`) parses as UTC midnight while the slash form (`new Date('2024/01/15')`) parses in local time — the two disagree by a day near timezone boundaries; a 10-digit literal passed to `new Date(...)` is a UNIX-SECONDS timestamp, but `Date` expects milliseconds and silently yields a 1970 date; and adding `86400000`ms (or `24*60*60*1000`) to a `Date`/`getTime()`/`.now()` value assumes every day is exactly 24 hours, which breaks across DST transitions (23/25-hour days). All three are correct in UTC-only systems — use a date library's `addDays`, or UTC-only arithmetic, when they aren't. Suppress a vetted case with `// date-pitfall-ok`.

`.forEach(async ...)` — `forEach` ignores the promises its callback returns: the loop call itself resolves before any callback body finishes, callback errors become unhandled promise rejections, and there is no way to await completion. Use a `for...of` loop with `await`, or `Promise.all(items.map(async ...))` if the iterations are independent. `.map(async ...)` is a related but different defect (an unawaited array of promises) covered separately by `be-reliability/await-in-map`. Suppress a vetted case with `// foreach-async-ok`.

`new Promise(async (resolve, reject) => ...)` — an async executor function's own thrown errors/rejections are swallowed, because the `Promise` constructor never observes the promise the async executor itself returns; it also usually signals a redundant wrapper, since the async function already returns a promise. Core ESLint's `no-async-promise-executor` already covers this when configured — zzop's value here is catching it with zero lint config. Suppress a vetted case with `// promise-async-exec-ok`.

`parseInt(...)` called with a single argument — the radix parameter documents intent and guards against legacy surprises (`'0x10'` auto-parses as hex; old engines treated a leading-zero string as octal). Pass an explicit radix, e.g. `parseInt(x, 10)`. This scan only looks at the literal argument span up to the closing `)`; a nested call (`parseInt(getVal())`) is not flagged — a documented miss rather than a guess. Core ESLint's `radix` rule covers this too but is not in its `recommended` config. Suppress a vetted case with `// parseint-radix-ok`.
